label: Egyptian
description: Monumental stone architecture with axial plans, massive columns, pylons, and symbolic royal or religious forms.

label: Pre-Columbian
description: Indigenous American traditions known for pyramids, ceremonial plazas, carved stonework, and astronomy-aligned layouts.

label: Sinosphere
description: East Asian architecture shaped by timber frames, courtyards, tiled roofs, symmetry, and modular planning.

label: Greek
description: Classical architecture centered on proportion, column orders, temples, stone entablatures, and civic spaces.

label: Roman
description: Engineering-driven architecture using arches, vaults, domes, concrete, basilicas, baths, and monumental public works.

label: Buddhist
description: Sacred architecture built around stupas, monasteries, rock-cut halls, pagodas, and symbolic paths of devotion.

label: Byzantine
description: Domed churches and centralized plans enriched with mosaics, pendentives, layered vaults, and luminous interiors.

label: Dravidian
description: South Indian temple architecture with towering gopurams, carved stone halls, enclosed courts, and sacred water tanks.

label: Himalayan
description: Mountain architecture combining monasteries, fortress-like forms, stacked volumes, painted ornament, and climate-aware construction.

label: Islamic
description: Architecture marked by courtyards, domes, minarets, geometric ornament, calligraphy, arches, and shaded spatial sequences.

label: Romanesque
description: Massive masonry churches with rounded arches, thick walls, barrel vaults, sturdy towers, and sculpted portals.

label: Khmer
description: Temple-mountain complexes with axial causeways, moats, corbelled galleries, sandstone carving, and cosmic symbolism.

label: Maru Gurjara
description: Western Indian temple style noted for intricate stone carving, clustered spires, ornate ceilings, and sculptural detail.

label: Gothic
description: Vertical architecture using pointed arches, rib vaults, flying buttresses, stained glass, and richly articulated facades.

label: Rennaisance
description: Renaissance architecture revived classical symmetry, proportion, domes, pilasters, and mathematically ordered spaces.

label: Baroque
description: Dramatic architecture with dynamic curves, grand stairways, theatrical light, rich ornament, and immersive spatial effects.

label: Rococo
description: Playful late Baroque interiors with asymmetry, pastel palettes, shell motifs, mirrors, gilding, and delicate ornament.

label: Neoclassical
description: Restrained classical revival emphasizing symmetry, columns, pediments, clean geometry, and archaeological references.

label: Beaux Arts
description: Academic architecture combining classical planning, grand composition, sculptural decoration, and monumental civic presence.

label: Art Nouveau
description: Organic modern style with flowing lines, floral motifs, ironwork, glass, and integrated decorative design.

label: Modern, International
description: Modernist architecture favoring functional plans, minimal ornament, open space, glass, steel, and concrete.

label: Brutalist
description: Bold modern architecture defined by exposed concrete, heavy massing, structural honesty, and sculptural forms.

label: Post-modern
description: Pluralistic architecture that reintroduced color, symbolism, historical references, irony, and playful forms.

label: Deconstrutivism
description: Fragmented contemporary architecture with skewed geometries, disrupted surfaces, expressive structure, and unstable-looking forms.
